{{Short description|Reformulation of classical mechanics using Hamiltonian formalism}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Hamiltonian mechanics
| image = 
| caption = 
| topics = [[Classical mechanics]], [[Lagrangian mechanics]], [[Phase space]], [[Conservation of energy]]
| key_equations = [[Hamilton's equations]]
}}

'''Hamiltonian mechanics''' is a mathematically sophisticated reformulation of [[classical mechanics]], introduced by the Irish mathematician [[William Rowan Hamilton]] in 1833. It describes a physical system in terms of [[generalized coordinate]]s <math>q_i</math> and their [[conjugate momentum|conjugate momenta]] <math>p_i</math>, together with a [[Hamiltonian (mechanics)|Hamiltonian function]] <math>H</math> that represents the total energy of the system.

== Hamilton's equations ==
The time evolution of the system is governed by [[Hamilton's equations]]:
:<math>\dot{q}_i = \frac{\partial H}{\partial p_i}, \qquad \dot{p}_i = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial q_i}</math>

These are <math>2n</math> first-order differential equations (compared to <math>n</math> second-order equations in Lagrangian mechanics), where <math>n</math> is the number of degrees of freedom.

== The Hamiltonian ==
For a conservative system, the Hamiltonian equals the total energy:
:<math>H(q, p) = T + V = \frac{p^2}{2m} + V(q)</math>

The time derivative of the Hamiltonian is:
:<math>\frac{dH}{dt} = \frac{\partial H}{\partial t}</math>

If the Hamiltonian has no explicit time dependence, energy is conserved.

== Phase space and symplectic geometry ==
{{Main|Phase space}}
The state of a system is represented as a point in the <math>2n</math>-dimensional [[phase space]] with coordinates <math>(q_1, \ldots, q_n, p_1, \ldots, p_n)</math>. The evolution of the system traces a trajectory in phase space. [[Liouville's theorem (Hamiltonian)|Liouville's theorem]] states that phase-space volume is conserved under Hamiltonian flow.

== Poisson brackets ==
The [[Poisson bracket]] of two functions <math>A(q,p)</math> and <math>B(q,p)</math> is:
:<math>\{A, B\} = \sum_i \left(\frac{\partial A}{\partial q_i}\frac{\partial B}{\partial p_i} - \frac{\partial A}{\partial p_i}\frac{\partial B}{\partial q_i}\right)</math>

The time evolution of any observable is given by:
:<math>\frac{dA}{dt} = \{A, H\} + \frac{\partial A}{\partial t}</math>

== Bridge to quantum mechanics ==
Hamiltonian mechanics provides the natural framework for transition to [[quantum mechanics]]:
* Poisson brackets → quantum commutators: <math>\{A, B\} \rightarrow \frac{1}{i\hbar}[\hat{A}, \hat{B}]</math>
* The [[Hamiltonian operator]] governs time evolution in the [[Schrödinger equation]]

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Classical mechanics]]
* [[Hamiltonian (quantum mechanics)]]
* [[Lagrangian mechanics]]
* [[Liouville's theorem (Hamiltonian)]]
* [[Noether's theorem]]
* [[Phase space]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Hamiltonian mechanics| ]]
[[Category:Classical mechanics]]
[[Category:Symplectic geometry]]
